questions.to_find: The asymptotic approximation of the return value of fun1(n)

understand.plain: The function has three nested loops. The outer loop runs n-1 times. The middle loop counts how many times n can be halved (giving floor(log_2(n))). The innermost loop runs by doubling k, so it runs floor(log_2(p)) times. q is incremented in the innermost loop, so its total count is (n-1) * floor(log_2(log_2(n))).

keywords.explain: The logarithm applied twice to n; grows extremely slowly.
A loop where the variable is multiplied by 2 each iteration runs in O(log(limit)) iterations.
keywords.example: k=1,2,4,8,... up to p takes ceil(log_2(p)) steps.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' viewBox='0 0 420 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='410' height='150' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Loop analysis of fun1(n):</text><text x='20' y='60' class='sub ink'>Outer loop (i): runs n-1 times   ->   O(n)</text><text x='20' y='85' class='sub ink'>Middle loop (j): halving n until 1  ->   p = floor(log_2(n))</text><text x='20' y='110' class='sub ink'>Inner loop (k): doubling until p  ->   floor(log_2(p)) = floor(log_2(log_2(n)))</text><text x='20' y='140' class='sub ink'>Total q increments = (n-1) * floor(log_2(log_2(n)))  ~  n*log(log n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three loops: outer runs n-1 times; middle counts log_2(n) halvings into p; inner doubles k from 1 to p, running log_2(p) = log(log n) times. Total = n * log(log n).

given.aim: Determine the asymptotic growth of the return value q of fun1(n).

terms.meaning: Number of times n can be integer-divided by 2 before reaching 1
terms.connects: p = floor(log_2(n)), used as the limit for the innermost loop

terms.meaning: k doubles each step (k=1,2,4,...) while k < p, giving floor(log_2(p)) steps
terms.connects: q is incremented once per inner-loop iteration

terms.meaning: i runs from 1 to n-1, giving n-1 iterations

given.plan: Find the number of iterations of each loop separately and multiply. The outer loop contributes n-1, the middle loop gives p = log_2(n), and the inner loop gives log_2(p) = log_2(log_2(n)). Multiply all to get Theta(n log(log n)).

steps.title: Analyze the middle loop: compute p
steps.apply: The loop 'for (j = n; j > 1; j = j/2) ++p' halves j repeatedly until j <= 1. Starting at n, after t halvings j = n/2^t. It stops when j <= 1, i.e., t = floor(log_2(n)). So p = floor(log_2(n)).
steps.note: p is the same for every iteration of the outer loop, so it can be computed once.

steps.title: Analyze the inner loop: count q increments per outer iteration
steps.apply: The loop 'for (k = 1; k < p; k = k * 2) ++q' doubles k each time: k=1,2,4,... until k >= p. Number of steps = floor(log_2(p)) = floor(log_2(log_2(n))).
steps.note: This is log applied twice to n.

steps.title: Multiply over all outer iterations
steps.apply: The outer loop runs n-1 times. In each iteration, the inner loop adds floor(log_2(log_2(n))) to q. Total: q = (n-1) * floor(log_2(log_2(n))) = Theta(n log(log n)).
steps.note: Among the given options, n log(log n) matches exactly.

solution.result: The return value is Theta(n log(log n)), so the answer is D. n log(log n).

questions.formula_note: Loop complexity analysis: outer O(n), middle O(log n) giving limit p, inner O(log p) = O(log log n); product = O(n log log n).

understand.plain: The function is recursive. For n=1 it returns 1. For larger n it sums products fun(k)*fun(n-k) for all k from 1 to n-1, then adds 1 (the initial value of x). Computing values bottom-up from fun(1) to fun(5) gives the answer.

keywords.explain: fun(n) = 1 + sum_{k=1}^{n-1} fun(k)*fun(n-k); this mirrors the Catalan number recurrence.
Compute fun(1), fun(2), ... in order so each result is reused.
keywords.example: fun(2) needs fun(1) which is already known = 1.

understand.visual_alt: Recursive tree computing fun(5) by expanding into sums of products of smaller fun values, computed bottom-up.

given.aim: Compute fun(5) by evaluating fun(1) through fun(5) iteratively.

terms.connects: Used in computing all higher values

terms.meaning: Recursive case: x starts at 1 and accumulates products
terms.connects: Each higher value depends on all smaller ones

terms.meaning: fun(k)*fun(n-k) appears twice for most k (once as k and once as n-k)
terms.example: For n=4, fun(1)*fun(3) and fun(3)*fun(1) both appear
terms.connects: Can double the partial sums to speed computation

given.plan: Compute fun(1), fun(2), fun(3), fun(4), fun(5) sequentially using the recurrence.

steps.title: Compute fun(1) through fun(4)
steps.note: fun values so far: 1, 2, 5, 15 (these are Catalan numbers C_0, C_1, C_2, C_3 scaled by initial 1).

questions.formula_note: Catalan-number-style recurrence: fun(n) = 1 + sum_{k=1}^{n-1} fun(k)*fun(n-k), computed iteratively bottom-up.

questions.question:
Suppose c is an array of length k, where all the entries are from the set {0, 1}. For any positive integers a and n, consider the following pseudocode:

DOSOMETHING(c, a, n)
  z = 1
  for i = 0 to k-1 do
    z = z^2 mod n
    if c[i] = 1 then z = (z * a) mod n
  return z

If k = 4, c = [1, 0, 1, 1], a = 2, and n = 8, then the output of DOSOMETHING(c, a, n) is ______.

A. 0
B. 2
C. 4
D. 6
questions.to_find: The output of DOSOMETHING([1,0,1,1], 2, 8) with k=4

understand.plain: The pseudocode implements left-to-right binary modular exponentiation. The array c represents the binary digits of an exponent (most significant bit first). The algorithm computes a^m mod n where m is the number whose binary representation is c. With c=[1,0,1,1], m=11 (binary 1011), so it computes 2^11 mod 8.

keywords.explain: Computing a^m mod n efficiently by repeated squaring and conditional multiplication.
c=[1,0,1,1] is the binary number 1011 = 11 in decimal; each bit tells whether to multiply by a.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='440' height='200' viewBox='0 0 440 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='430' height='190' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>DOSOMETHING trace: a=2, n=8, c=[1,0,1,1]</text><text x='20' y='58' class='sub ink'>z=1 (start)</text><text x='20' y='80' class='sub ink'>i=0: z=1^2 mod 8=1; c[0]=1: z=(1*2) mod 8 = 2</text><text x='20' y='102' class='sub ink'>i=1: z=2^2 mod 8=4; c[1]=0: no multiply, z=4</text><text x='20' y='124' class='sub ink'>i=2: z=4^2 mod 8=16 mod 8=0; c[2]=1: z=(0*2) mod 8=0</text><text x='20' y='146' class='sub ink'>i=3: z=0^2 mod 8=0; c[3]=1: z=(0*2) mod 8=0</text><text x='20' y='175' class='lbl ink'>return z = 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step-by-step trace: z starts at 1, after 4 iterations with c=[1,0,1,1], a=2, n=8, z becomes 0 (since 2^11 mod 8 = 2048 mod 8 = 0).

given.aim: Simulate DOSOMETHING with k=4, c=[1,0,1,1], a=2, n=8 step by step.

terms.meaning: Binary digits of exponent m=11 (binary 1011), MSB first
terms.connects: Each c[i] controls whether to multiply by a in that step
Doubles the exponent built so far

terms.example: 16 mod 8 = 0, so once z reaches a multiple of 8 it stays 0
terms.connects: 2^3=8=0 mod 8, so 2^m mod 8 = 0 for all m >= 3

given.plan: Trace the 4 iterations of the loop, updating z at each step by squaring and optionally multiplying by a=2, all mod 8.

steps.title: Trace the loop iteration by iteration
steps.formula_raw: z = z^2 mod n; if c[i]=1: z = (z*a) mod n
steps.note: Once z becomes 0 (a multiple of 8), it stays 0 for all subsequent steps.

steps.title: Verify using direct computation
steps.apply:
c=[1,0,1,1] in binary = 1011 base 2 = 11 in decimal.
2^11 = 2048.
2048 / 8 = 256 exactly, so 2048 mod 8 = 0.
steps.note: For any m >= 3, 2^m mod 8 = 0, confirming the result.

questions.formula_note: Left-to-right binary modular exponentiation: c[] encodes the exponent in binary (MSB first); the algorithm computes a^m mod n where m is the integer with binary representation c.

questions.answer: D. It will not print anything and will not terminate
questions.to_find: What happens when convert is called with a positive integer n

understand.plain: The function tries to print the binary representation by recursing on n/2 before printing n%2. However, when n reaches 0, n/2 = 0, so convert(0) calls convert(0) again — causing infinite recursion that never returns. Since printf is only executed after the recursive call returns, nothing is ever printed.

keywords.explain: convert(0) calls convert(0/2) = convert(0), which is the same call, looping forever.
printf comes after the recursive call, so it can only execute when recursion returns — which never happens due to infinite loop.
keywords.example: convert(5) calls convert(2) first, and only prints 1 after convert(2) returns — but it never does.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='410' height='190' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>convert(5) call chain:</text><text x='20' y='55' class='sub ink'>convert(5) -> convert(2) -> convert(1) -> convert(0) -+</text><text x='20' y='78' class='sub ink'>                                                       |</text><text x='20' y='100' class='sub ink'>convert(0): n=0, not < 0, so calls convert(0/2)=convert(0) <--+</text><text x='20' y='130' class='sub mut'>Infinite loop: convert(0) never returns!</text><text x='20' y='158' class='no sub'>printf never executes => nothing printed, no termination</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Call chain: convert(5)->convert(2)->convert(1)->convert(0)->convert(0)->... infinite loop. printf after recursive call never executes.

given.aim: Trace convert(n) for a positive integer n and determine if it terminates and what it prints.

terms.meaning: Only negative n causes printf to run immediately without recursion
terms.connects: Positive integers and 0 never hit this base case

terms.meaning: When n=0: 0 is not < 0, so it goes to else branch; calls convert(0/2) = convert(0) again
terms.connects: Any positive n eventually leads to convert(0) via repeated halving, causing infinite loop

terms.meaning: printf("%d", n%2) is placed after the recursive call; it only runs when the recursion returns
terms.example: convert(1): calls convert(0) first; printf(1%2) only after convert(0) returns — it never does
terms.connects: Since convert(0) never returns, no printf is ever executed

given.plan: Trace the recursion: any positive n halves to 0; convert(0) recurses on convert(0) infinitely. Since printf is post-recursion, nothing is printed and the function never terminates.

steps.title: Trace call stack for n=5
steps.apply:
convert(5): n=5 >= 0, calls convert(5/2) = convert(2), then would print 5%2=1
convert(2): n=2 >= 0, calls convert(2/2) = convert(1), then would print 2%2=0
convert(1): n=1 >= 0, calls convert(1/2) = convert(0), then would print 1%2=1
convert(0): n=0 >= 0 (not < 0!), calls convert(0/2) = convert(0), then would print 0%2=0
=> convert(0) calls itself again => infinite recursion
steps.note: The bug: n=0 is not handled as a base case. The condition 'n < 0' does NOT catch n=0.

steps.title: Determine output behavior
steps.apply: Since convert(0) recurses forever without returning, the call stack never unwinds. The printf statements in convert(0), convert(1), convert(2), convert(5) are all after the recursive call — they are reached only when recursion returns. Since recursion never returns, no printf is ever executed. The function runs forever without printing anything.
steps.note: If the condition were 'n <= 0' or 'n < 2', the function would correctly print binary and terminate.

solution.result: The function will not print anything and will not terminate. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Missing base case for n=0 in binary-print recursion causes infinite loop; post-order printf placement means nothing is printed before the infinite loop takes over.
